body.content:
Shinjuku San-Chome
Shinjuku’s Golden Gai and Kabukicho entertainment districts are world famous. Only a few steps away, the Shinjuku San-Chome district is almost completely unknown, yet its one of the city’s most enjoyable nightlife districts. Here James Catchpole takes you on a short wander.
![Intersection of Shinjuku-dori Street and Meiji-dori Street](https://photos.smugmug.com/Shinjuku-San-Chome/i-trbptwV/0/L/IMG_5639-L.jpg "Intersection of Shinjuku-dori Street and Meiji-dori Street")
Intersection of Shinjuku-dori Street and Meiji-dori Street - image © James Catchpole
Visitors to Tokyo often get quickly overwhelmed by the alleyways, lights and bustle of [Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/shinjuku/). It can be an adventure just getting out the station with more than 200 exits to navigate, let alone finding a particular shop or restaurant once on street level. As a result many people tend to stop their exploring once they’ve seen the famous [Kabukicho](https://trulytokyo.com/kabukicho/) red-light district, and the old drinking den maze of the [Golden Gai](https://trulytokyo.com/golden-gai/). Yet just another few minutes walk east of those great areas is the less famous but equally fun Shinjuku San-Chome neighborhood. It’s a dense, square-block area lined with all types of eateries, karaoke joints and some of the best music bars in Tokyo.
Top Activities In Tokyo
[![Two sumo wrestlers spa as part of a morning practice session in Tokyo, Japan](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Two sumo wrestlers spa as part of a morning practice session in Tokyo, Japan")](https://trulytokyo.com/visiting-a-morning-sumo-practice-in-tokyo/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-01)[Morning Sumo
Practice](https://trulytokyo.com/visiting-a-morning-sumo-practice-in-tokyo/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-02)See sumo wrestlers
engaged in rigorous
morning practice[More Info](https://trulytokyo.com/visiting-a-morning-sumo-practice-in-tokyo/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-03)
[![A group of silhouetted visitors stand in front of colourful, geometric projections at teamLab Borderless in Tokyo, Japan](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E "A group of silhouetted visitors stand in front of colourful, geometric projections at teamLab Borderless in Tokyo, Japan")](https://trulytokyo.com/teamlab-borderless/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-01)[teamLab
Borderless](https://trulytokyo.com/teamlab-borderless/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-02)Art without limits
at teamLab Borderless
in Azabudai Hills[More Info](https://trulytokyo.com/teamlab-borderless/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-03)
[![The view of Mt. Fuji against a bright sky from Tenjoyama Park outside Tokyo, Japan](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E "The view of Mt. Fuji against a bright sky from Tenjoyama Park outside Tokyo, Japan")](https://trulytokyo.com/a-day-trip-to-the-mt-fuji-area-lake-kawaguchiko/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-01)[Day Trip To
Mount Fuji](https://trulytokyo.com/a-day-trip-to-the-mt-fuji-area-lake-kawaguchiko/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-02)Stunning views of
Mount Fuji from
Lake Kawaguchiko[More Info](https://trulytokyo.com/a-day-trip-to-the-mt-fuji-area-lake-kawaguchiko/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-03)
[![A traditional red Japanese float with taiko drummers on stage at Samurai Restaurant Time in Tokyo](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E "A traditional red Japanese float with taiko drummers on stage at Samurai Restaurant Time in Tokyo")](https://trulytokyo.com/samurai-restaurant-time-a-review-and-guide-to-getting-tickets/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-01)[Samurai Restaurant
Time Show](https://trulytokyo.com/samurai-restaurant-time-a-review-and-guide-to-getting-tickets/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-02)Essential Tokyo
cabaret show with fun,
kitschy extravagance[More Info](https://trulytokyo.com/samurai-restaurant-time-a-review-and-guide-to-getting-tickets/?utm_source=grid&utm_medium=link&utm_id=TT-Activities-Grid-03)
It’s super easy to find: As you walk east along Shinjuku-dori Street away from the My City exit of Shinjuku Station you’ll come to the intersection of Shinjuku-dori Street and Meiji-dori Street, seeing Louis Vuitton (LV) on the left. This is less than a five-minute walk.
Cross the street and next to LV you’ll see the blue and white sign for Shinjuku San-Chome subway station (bottom left corner of the next photo).
![Shinjuku San-chome Subway Station](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Shinjuku San-chome Subway Station")
Shinjuku San-chome Subway Station - image © James Catchpole
Just to the right of the station steps is a small street that takes you into the west side of the Shinjuku San-Chome neighborhood. Walk down into the alley for just 15 seconds and you’ll see this Sakaezushi sushi restaurant on the left.
![Sakaezushi Restaurant ](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Sakaezushi Restaurant ")
Sakaezushi Restaurant - image © James Catchpole
Sushi restaurants can be a bit intimidating for visitors to Japan, with various challenges like forbiddingly stern staff, exorbitant prices or lack of an English menu. Sakaezushi has no such problems and there’s an English menu sign right out front with the prices listed. This is a [proper sushi place](https://trulytokyo.com/best-sushi-in-tokyo/) (ie, not conveyor-belt sushi). If you grab a counter seat you can watch the chefs in action as they prepare each dish.
##
Check Hotel Availability
###
Destination
#### Check-in date
#### Check-out date
![Booking.com](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='150'%20height='0'%20viewBox='0%200%20150%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
If you continue past Sakaezushi you’ll get to the center of San-Chome. Look to the right and you’ll see signs reading ‘Suehiro-Dori’ above from the Shinjuku-dori entrance.
![Entrance to Suehiro-dori Street](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Entrance to Suehiro-dori Street")
Entrance to Suehiro-dori Street - image © James Catchpole
Look left and you’ll see the main central street of the neighborhood, which is narrow and packed with dozens of appealing spots.
![The main street of Shinjuku-San-chome](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "The main street of Shinjuku-San-chome")
The main street of Shinjuku-San-chome - image © James Catchpole
Take 10 steps and immediately on the left is an example of the old-style drinking dens that used to be everywhere in post-war Tokyo, before rapid economic development and changing tastes saw many of them closed down. There is now a nostalgia for these old joints and recent years have seen many of them revive themselves with younger customers.
![Old style ‘tachi-nommi’ stand & drink jointOld style ‘tachi-nommi’ stand & drink joint](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Old style ‘tachi-nommi’ stand & drink jointOld style ‘tachi-nommi’ stand & drink joint")
Old style ‘tachi-nommi’ stand & drink joint - image © James Catchpole
Under the red and white sign reading Shinjuku San-Chome Horumon Yokocho, behind the plastic curtain (not there in the warm months) are four separate establishments, one behind another, which serve a variety of grilled snacks and small hot-pots with copious amounts of fairly cheap booze. Cramped, earthy and quite smoky, this is the kind of place Tokyoites pour into after work to relax over some beers and small bites that are tastier than you’d expect. They’re called ‘tachi nomiya’ (‘stand and drink shop’)
This particular group of four are fairly used to having foreigners pop in (unlike some of the really older ones) so don’t hesitate to enter, they’ll be kind enough to help you with the menu. Don’t expect the famed Japanese service in this kind of joint – here, it’s all about noise, crowds and lots and lots of drinks.
Back on the main road right past the old drinking den is a famous old vaudeville and rakugo (storytelling) theater called Suehiro-Tei.
![Famous old vaudeville theater](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Famous old vaudeville theater")
Famous old vaudeville theater - image © James Catchpole
Shows are only in Japanese so you can probably give this place a miss unless you understand Japanese or are happy to just check out the vibe.
Walk just 30 seconds past the theatre and on both of the street you will find bars, international restaurants and many types of ‘[izakaya](https://trulytokyo.com/best-izakaya-and-sake-specialists-in-tokyo/)’ (Japanese sit-down pubs). There are usually staff standing outside with menus and info on set meal plans, so feel free to check the menu before heading in anywhere. Unlike the street touts in many countries, the staff holding menus here are not going to lie to you about the pricing. (Note: this does not apply to ‘other’ types of establishments in Kabukicho or [Roppongi](https://trulytokyo.com/roppongi-and-akasaka/)!)
And remember to keep looking up! There are places on the second and third floors of these buildings that may catch your eye. (A good rule in Tokyo: Always look up as some of the best spots to eat and drink are on the upper floors of buildings – and they’re usually cheaper than ground-level places.)
If the older standing drinking den you just passed wasn’t for you then the ‘yakitori’ (grilled chicken) izakaya called [Shosuke](https://trulytokyo.com/shousuke/) a little bit up on the left is recommended.
![Yakitori (grilled chicken) izakaya](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Yakitori (grilled chicken) izakaya")
Yakitori (grilled chicken) izakaya - image © James Catchpole
There are both tables and chairs and tatami-mat sit-on-the-floor areas, so if you want to rest while munching and drinking then this is a nice spot. Be forewarned there is NO space between you and your neighboring customers, and if they’re smoking you’re going to share each cigarette with them.
Walk a little past the yakitori place and you come to a small intersection in the middle of the San-Chome block. On the left corner you’ll see this shop, Maruko Suisan.
![Maruko Suisan seafood restaurant](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Maruko Suisan seafood restaurant")
Maruko Suisan seafood restaurant - image © James Catchpole
Maruko Suisan is a cheap but very high-quality seafood joint with a rotating menu of specials (last time I was there they featured several dishes from Hokkaido, the northernmost island in Japan which is well known for it’s top notch seafood) as well as the usual sashimi or grilled fish. It’s a no frills spot with rather uncomfortable seats but the service is quick and the food extra good. They get bonus points for a nice happy hour until 7pm, making it a good spot to drop by early if you’re starting off a heavy session. We had a substantial meal there last time with about 3 or 4 beers apiece for about ￥4000 (about US$40)
Turning across from the Maruko Suisan (丸港 水産) you’ll turn right and see the tobacco shop on the left corner.
![Corner Smoke Shop is a good landmark in the center of San-Chome](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Corner Smoke Shop is a good landmark in the center of San-Chome")
Corner Smoke Shop is a good landmark in the center of San-Chome - image © James Catchpole
Walk past the smoke shop keeping it on your left, and just two buildings down you’ll see this building.
![Always keep a lookout, even the dumpiest looking buildings can have great places.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Always keep a lookout, even the dumpiest looking buildings can have great places.")
Always keep a lookout, even the dumpiest looking buildings can have great places. - image © James Catchpole
It’s a narrow dumpy building that has two of the best bars in all of Shinjuku. On the second floor is Bar Box, run by Aoki-san, a very welcoming host who in between making drinks puts on vinyl from the extensive collection behind the bar, all funky/groovy jazz albums. There’s no ‘table charge’ at Bar Box (unlike most bars of this type in Japan) and Aoki-san is very friendly, always happy to communicate as best he can in English. Sometimes the small music bars around town can be a bit difficult to enter for tourists but you can enter Bar Box with no fear.
Nor do you need to worry about entering the third floor joint, Esogie Africa Bar. Owner Lucky-san is from Nigeria and is arguably the friendliest bar owner in Tokyo. He’s been in business for 13 years, serving drinks (including some Nigerian home brew spirit called ogogoro) while cooking up some authentic West African food, with the background music being fantastic tunes from Nigeria and other African countries. (I always write down the CDs playing when in there, it’s helped build my African music collection). Lucky has made a real effort to show that Africans in Japan are not what the media here usually portrays, going out of his way to teach customers about Nigerian culture. It may not be first on your itinerary in Japan to go to an African bar, but Esogie is a great spot for a drink and meal, a rare spot for locals and African expats to hang together.
![Bar Box & Esogie](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Bar Box & Esogie")
Bar Box & Esogie - image © James Catchpole
There is so much more though on each of the tiny intersecting alleys of San-Chome. If you walk just past the Bar Box and Esogie building look right and you’ll see this.
![Look down any alleyway and see more places to eat & drink](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Look down any alleyway and see more places to eat & drink")
Look down any alleyway and see more places to eat & drink - image © James Catchpole
On warm evenings each of the shops on this alley have seats spilling outside onto the street, making the atmosphere lively and fun, but much less intense than drinking nearer to Shinjuku Station. San-Chome has a more chilled-out vibe (likely the result of all the music bars, which number more than 25 in the neighborhood from jazz to soul to rock to blues to Cuban to reggae).
If you continue past the alley turn right at the end of the street, and you’ll be along the eastern edge of San-Chome. Just 10 steps up on the right you’ll see the first-floor sign for Soul Stream.
![7000 soul music records inside Soul Stream](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "7000 soul music records inside Soul Stream")
7000 soul music records inside Soul Stream - image © James Catchpole
Soul Stream is a small square room, dominated by the wall of vinyl albums along the wall on the right. Owner Ooki-san smoothly stands behind the turntables playing record after record from his wonderful collection. You can sit here for two hours chatting, drinking and listening to superb music in a totally unpretentious atmosphere.
If you keep walking down past Soul Stream you’ll see the Tokyu Stay hotel on the right, a fairly new addition to the neighborhood. Across from the hotel if you look up to the 4th floor on the left you’ll see a sign with a black circle and yellow letters saying ‘Curtis’.
![Bar Curtis, one of the best bars in all of Tokyo for music fans.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Bar Curtis, one of the best bars in all of Tokyo for music fans.")
Bar Curtis, one of the best bars in all of Tokyo for music fans. - image © James Catchpole
This is always my last stop of the evening as owner Abe-san usually doesn’t open until about 8:30 or 9pm. Curtis is simply an amazing bar: lots of vinyl, some funky rattan furniture, and a small roof top patio open in the summer, with speakers pumping in the tunes. If you’re there on a weekend you may even find a DJ event going on, but the vibe is not clubbish at all, it’s casual and warm. Just the perfect way to end an evening.
While there are more visitors coming to San-Chome in recent years, it still doesn’t see the kind of traffic that Kabukicho or Golden Gai do, two areas that have been very thoroughly documented in the international media. Yet I find San-Chome to be the most fun of any area in town. It has the food options, the drinking spots and more than enough great music bars to cater to a multitude of tastes. Yet it’s also very navigable: a walk around the perimeter takes maybe 15 minutes, leaving plenty of time to cut through the intersecting alleys. There are easily close to 100 eateries and bars to choose from in the area so I’d advise walking around and getting oriented first, keeping track of the signs that pique your interest. Then circle back and start your evening of good food and drink in one of Tokyo’s coolest neighborhoods. You may even end up here at Ryu-Nosu, a grilled bbq place (with a lot more on the menu) just because the entry is so sweet looking.
![With an entry like that, you know the place will be fun.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "With an entry like that, you know the place will be fun.")
With an entry like that, you know the place will be fun. - image © James Catchpole
**Near To Here:**
Shinjuku San-Chome is located in [Tokyo's Shinjuku district](https://trulytokyo.com/shinjuku/). See our complete list of [things to do in Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/shinjuku/), including places to eat, nightlife and places to stay.
**About**
James Catchpole is a broadcaster and music writer based in Japan since 1997. He runs [www.tokyojazzsite.com](https://www.tokyojazzsite.com) and hosts the online radio program Tokyo Jazz Map, and the OK Jazz Podcast. He’s visited over 200 music bars throughout the Tokyo metropolitan area.
### Tokyo Vacation Checklist
* [ESSENTIALS](https://trulytokyo.com/first-time-in-tokyo/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-01) – For all the essentials in a brief overview, see my [First Time In Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/first-time-in-tokyo/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-02) guide
* [HOTELS](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-Checklist-03) – Check Tokyo accommodation availability and pricing on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-Checklist-04) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?pcs=1&cid=1743457&city=5085&tag=TT-Checklist-05) - often you can book with no upfront payment and free cancellation
* [WHERE TO STAY](https://trulytokyo.com/where-to-stay-in-tokyo/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-06) – Need tips on where to stay? See my one page guide [Where To Stay In Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/where-to-stay-in-tokyo/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-07)
* [BULLET TRAIN](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=827006&k_site=https://www.klook.com/en-US/rails-32/1012-japan/) – You can buy [shinkansen (bullet train) tickets online](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=827006&k_site=https://www.klook.com/en-US/rails-32/1012-japan/) from Klook - popular routes include [Tokyo to Kyoto](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=827006&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1305/tokyo-to-kyoto-trains/), [Tokyo to Osaka](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=827006&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1304/tokyo-to-shin-osaka-trains/) and [Tokyo to Hiroshima](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=827006&k_site=https://www.klook.com/rails-32/1012-japan/route-1310/tokyo-to-hiroshima-trains/)
* [SIM CARDS](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=877200&k_site=https://www.klook.com/search/result/?query=japan%20esim) – You can [buy an eSim](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=2652&aff_adid=877200&k_site=https://www.klook.com/search/result/?query=japan%20esim) to activate in Japan or [buy a Japan SIM card online](<https://www.getyourguide.com/s/?q=tokyo sim&searchSource=8&partner_id=LAMDN2W&utm_medium=online_publisher&placement=content-middle&cmp=TT-Checklist-08>) for collection on arrival at Tokyo Narita or Haneda airports. Or rent an [unlimited data pocket wifi router](<https://www.getyourguide.com/s/?q=japan wifi&searchSource=7&partner_id=LAMDN2W&utm_medium=online_publisher&placement=content-middle&cmp=TT-Checklist-09>)
* [PACKING LIST](https://insideosaka.com/packing-list-for-japan/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-10) – See my comprehensive [Packing List For Japan](https://insideosaka.com/packing-list-for-japan/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-11)
* [FLIGHTS](https://www.skyscanner.net/g/referrals/v1/flights/cheap-flights-to?adId=1100662&campaignId=13416&destination=tyoa&mediaPartnerId=1924024) – Compare airline flight prices and timings for [the best Japan flight deals](https://www.skyscanner.net/g/referrals/v1/flights/cheap-flights-to?adId=1100662&campaignId=13416&destination=tyoa&mediaPartnerId=1924024). Check my guides to [arriving at Narita Airport](https://trulytokyo.com/arriving-at-tokyos-narita-international-airport/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-12) and [at Haneda Airport](https://trulytokyo.com/arriving-at-tokyo-haneda-international-airport/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-13).
* [JAPAN RAIL PASS](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=616549&k_site=https://www.klook.com/activity/1420-7-day-whole-japan-rail-pass-jr-pass/) – If you're visiting more than one city, you might save money with a [Japan Rail Pass](https://affiliate.klook.com/redirect?aid=1591&aff_adid=616549&k_site=https://www.klook.com/activity/1420-7-day-whole-japan-rail-pass-jr-pass/) – see if [it's worth it](https://trulytokyo.com/japan-rail-pass-how-to-get-it/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-14) for you
* [TRAVEL INSURANCE](https://www.tkqlhce.com/click-7919142-15371527) – [World Nomads](https://www.tkqlhce.com/click-7919142-15371527) offers simple and flexible travel insurance. Buy at home or while traveling and claim online from anywhere in the world
* [WANT HELP?](https://trulytokyo.com/japan-travel-consulting/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-15) – Do you want help planning your trip? Chris Rowthorn and his team of Japan experts at [Japan Travel Consulting](https://trulytokyo.com/japan-travel-consulting/?utm_source=checkbox&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=TT-Checklist-16) can help
